architecture.summary: A classification pipeline that extracts pretrained Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) features from frozen Gemma 2 LLM hidden states, applies pooling and binarization transformations, and trains logistic regression classifiers on the resulting feature vectors. SAE activations from selected layers are uprojected to sparse dimensions, aggregated via summation or top-k selection, optionally thresholded to binary form, then fed to downstream task-specific classifiers.

biblio.title: Sparse Autoencoder Features for Classifications and Transferability

claims.statement: SAE-derived features achieve macro F1 > 0.8, outperforming hidden-state and BoW baselines
Larger models (9B, 9B-IT) achieve consistently higher mean performance (above 0.85 F1) compared to the 2B model
Middle-layer SAE features typically produce higher F1 scores than early- or late-layer features
Binarized and no max pooling of SAE features outperform both hidden-state probes and bag-of-words

baseline.name: hidden states and mean-diff top-n selection

claims.dataset: multilingual toxicity detection with 0.1–1.0 training set sampling
claims.statement: SAE features achieve superior F1 scores (0.85-0.90) compared to both hidden states (0.80-0.85) and top-n selection (0.75-0.80) under varying training data sampling rates

claims.dataset: multilingual toxicity detection (English to ES, RU, DE)
claims.statement: Cross-lingual transfer from English achieves 15-20% F1 score decrease compared to native training

claims.dataset: entity-based knowledge conflicts in QA (NQ-Swap, inspect_evals)
claims.statement: Smaller model (2B) SAE features can predict larger model (9B-IT) correctness with F1 scores above 80% with correct context, dropping 20% with context manipulation

Study is restricted to SAEs trained with Jump ReLU activation on Gemma 2 models as they were the only open-source models available with SAEs across varying layers, widths, and model sizes, potentially limiting generalizability to other model architectures and training paradigms
